01_initial_request.target_name: Synchronization-Safe Dynamic Microgrid Formation for DER-Led Distribution System Restoration via Constraint-Aware Graph Learning
01_initial_request.input_data: Modified IEEE 123-node test feeder with distributed energy resources (DERs) including dispatchable distributed generators (DDGs) and battery energy storage systems (BESSs); network topology data (switch states, line impedances, node connections); load profiles with priority classifications; DER capacity and location data; radiality and connectivity constraints for distribution network; synchronization safety constraints (voltage magnitude matching, frequency matching, phase angle alignment for MG interconnection); dynamic system mode and system class definitions for restorable states.
01_initial_request.user_intent: To address the critical gap in post-disaster distribution system restoration where existing dynamic microgrid (MG) formation methods lack synchronization safety constraints, potentially leading to infeasible or unstable restoration operations. Existing optimization-based approaches suffer from extremely high computational complexity and poor scalability for real-time applications. A fast, learning-based approach is needed that can generate synchronization-safe dynamic MG formation strategies with both computational efficiency and feasibility guarantees.
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: 1) Achieve synchronization-safe MG formation by ensuring all dynamic MG state transitions satisfy voltage, frequency, and phase angle constraints; 2) Restore maximum possible load through dynamic MG formation sequences; 3) Achieve significant computational speedup (orders of magnitude) over conventional MILP solvers for real-time applicability; 4) Maintain feasibility of generated solutions through differentiable constraint resolution; 5) Validate on modified IEEE 123-node feeder under multiple disaster scenarios.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: After major disasters, distribution systems experience prolonged outages. DERs (dispatchable DGs, BESSs) can form dynamic microgrids to restore critical loads incrementally. The concept of system mode and system class has been introduced to characterize the restorable states of the network, where system mode captures the energization status of nodes and system class defines feasible operating regions. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Existing dynamic MG formation methods treat the restoration as a pure optimization problem without explicitly modeling synchronization safety constraints. When two energized MGs are to be merged or a new DER is to be connected, the voltage magnitude difference, frequency deviation, and phase angle mismatch must be within tight tolerances, but these constraints are absent from current formulations. [Decision] To establish the theoretical foundation for synchronization-safe restoration, defined the complete mathematical model of dynamic MG formation with synchronization constraints. Formally defined system mode S^t = {s_1^t, s_2^t, ..., s_N^t} where s_i^t in {0,1} indicates node energization, and system class C^t representing the set of feasible operating points. Defined valid state transitions: (S^t, C^t) -> (S^{t+1}, C^{t+1}) is valid only if synchronization safety constraints are satisfied.

tool.name: Mathematical modeling and formal constraint definition

parameters.system_mode_definition: S^t = {s_1^t, ..., s_N^t}, s_i^t in {0,1}, where s_i^t = 1 means node i is energized at time t
parameters.system_class_definition: C^t = {(V, theta, P, Q) : power flow equations satisfied, operational limits met, synchronization constraints hold}
parameters.synchronization_constraints: |V_i^t - V_j^t| <= delta_V_max, |f_i^t - f_j^t| <= delta_f_max, |theta_i^t - theta_j^t| <= delta_theta_max for any two nodes being connected
parameters.transition_validity: Transition (S^t, C^t) -> (S^{t+1}, C^{t+1}) is valid iff: (1) new energized nodes can be supplied, (2) radiality maintained, (3) synchronization constraints hold at all interconnection points

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The formal model reveals that synchronization safety imposes three categories of constraints on every state transition: (1) voltage magnitude matching at interconnection points, (2) frequency matching across merged MGs, and (3) phase angle alignment within tolerances. The system class C^t effectively defines a feasible operating region that shrinks when synchronization constraints are violated, preventing invalid state transitions. This formulation transforms the dynamic MG formation problem from an unconstrained sequential optimization into a constraint-satisficing sequential decision problem.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The mathematical framework for synchronization-safe dynamic MG formation has been established with formal definitions of system mode, system class, and valid state transitions under synchronization constraints. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The constraint-aware sequential decision problem is combinatorial in nature and computationally intractable for real-time applications when solved by conventional MILP solvers. The state space grows exponentially with the number of nodes, and synchronization constraints add non-convex coupling between time steps. A tractable reformulation is needed that can leverage graph learning. [Decision] To make the problem tractable for graph neural networks, reformulated the dynamic MG formation as a constrained graph sequence generation problem. Each restoration step is a graph topology decision (which switches to close, which nodes to energize), and the constraint-aware STGCN must learn to generate valid graph sequences. Designed a spatio-temporal graph convolutional network (STGCN) architecture that captures both the spatial network topology (through graph convolution) and temporal state evolution (through temporal convolution).

parameters.spatial_layer: Graph convolution with adjacency matrix A encoding network topology; node features include DER status, load priority, voltage levels, and synchronization safety indicators
parameters.temporal_layer: 1D convolution along time dimension to capture state evolution patterns across restoration steps
parameters.input_features: Node features: [DER_capacity, load_priority, voltage_estimate, energization_status, sync_safety_flag]; Edge features: [line_impedance, switch_status, power_flow_capacity]
parameters.output: Probability distribution over valid switch operations (node energization actions) for each restoration step
parameters.architecture_details: Multi-layer STGCN with residual connections, batch normalization, and attention mechanism for critical node identification

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The STGCN architecture successfully encodes the distribution network topology as a graph and captures temporal dependencies between restoration steps. The spatial graph convolution learns local network patterns (e.g., DER neighborhoods, load clusters), while the temporal convolution captures the sequential nature of MG formation. Key observation: adding synchronization safety indicators as node features allows the network to learn constraint-aware representations that naturally avoid infeasible state transitions.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The STGCN architecture has been designed to generate dynamic MG formation sequences as graph topology decisions. However, the raw STGCN output is a continuous probability distribution over actions and does not guarantee feasibility of the generated solution. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The generated solutions may violate synchronization constraints, power flow equations, or radiality requirements. Traditional approaches use post-hoc repair or penalty functions, but these either fail to guarantee feasibility or slow down training. A principled mechanism is needed to embed constraint satisfaction directly into the learning pipeline. [Decision] To enforce constraint satisfaction, designed a differentiable feasibility resolution layer based on the straight-through estimator (STE). The STE layer maps infeasible continuous outputs to the nearest feasible discrete solution during forward pass while maintaining gradient flow during backward pass. Synchronization constraints (voltage, frequency, phase angle) are encoded as penalty functions within the STE layer, and the layer performs projected gradient descent to find the nearest feasible point.

parameters.ste_mechanism: Forward pass: round continuous outputs to discrete actions + project to feasible set; Backward pass: pass gradients through unchanged (straight-through)
parameters.synchronization_penalty: L_sync = w_V * |V_i - V_j|^2 + w_f * |f_i - f_j|^2 + w_theta * |theta_i - theta_j|^2 for interconnection nodes
parameters.feasibility_projection: Iterative projection onto constraint set: power balance, line capacity, voltage limits, radiality, synchronization safety
parameters.training_objective: Loss = -restored_load + lambda_1 * constraint_violation + lambda_2 * sync_safety_violation + lambda_3 * L2_regularization

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The STE-based feasibility layer successfully bridges the gap between continuous learning and discrete optimization. During training, the STGCN learns to produce outputs that are already close to feasible solutions, minimizing the projection distance. The synchronization safety penalties effectively train the network to avoid unsafe MG merging operations. The straight-through gradient estimator enables end-to-end training despite the non-differentiable discrete projection step.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The STGCN with STE-based feasibility layer has been designed to generate constraint-aware MG formation sequences. However, training from scratch requires large amounts of labeled data (optimal restoration sequences), which is expensive to generate via MILP solvers. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The learning efficiency needs improvement. High-quality warm-start solutions can significantly reduce the solution space the network needs to explore. Need a strategy to bootstrap the STGCN with good initial solutions. [Decision] To improve training efficiency, designed a partial warm-start generation mechanism that uses the STGCN to produce high-quality initial solutions (partial sequences of restoration actions) that are then refined by the feasibility layer. The partial warm-start covers the first k restoration steps with known-good solutions from offline MILP solving, and the STGCN learns to complete the remaining steps. This curriculum learning approach progressively reduces k as training advances.

parameters.warm_start_source: Offline MILP solutions from Gurobi for the first k restoration steps
parameters.curriculum_strategy: Start with k = T (full warm-start), progressively decrease k to 0 (no warm-start)
parameters.mip_formulation: Mixed-integer linear programming with synchronization constraints, radiality constraints, and power flow linearization
parameters.training_phases: Phase 1: Learn from complete MILP solutions; Phase 2: Learn to extend partial solutions; Phase 3: Generate complete solutions independently

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The partial warm-start strategy dramatically accelerates training convergence. In Phase 1, the STGCN quickly learns basic restoration patterns from complete MILP solutions. In Phase 2, it learns to extend partial solutions, developing planning capability. In Phase 3, the network generates complete solutions from scratch, achieving quality comparable to MILP but with orders-of-magnitude faster inference time. The generated warm-start solutions cover approximately 80% of the final optimal restored load, providing excellent initialization for the feasibility layer refinement.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The complete learning pipeline (STGCN + STE feasibility layer + partial warm-start) has been designed and trained. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The entire framework needs comprehensive validation on a realistic test system to assess: (1) restoration performance (load restored), (2) synchronization safety (constraint satisfaction rate), (3) computational efficiency (speedup vs. MILP), and (4) scalability. [Decision] To validate the framework, conducted case studies on the modified IEEE 123-node test feeder with realistic disaster scenarios. Designed multiple test cases with varying damage patterns, DER configurations, and load priorities. Compared against baseline MILP solver (Gurobi) and learning-based methods without synchronization constraints.

parameters.test_system: Modified IEEE 123-node feeder with 5 DERs (3 DDGs + 2 BESSs), 98 load nodes with priority classifications
parameters.disaster_scenarios: Multiple damage patterns: (1) single-line fault, (2) multi-line fault with substation isolation, (3) widespread damage with limited DER access
parameters.baselines: Baseline 1: Full MILP (Gurobi) with synchronization constraints; Baseline 2: STGCN without STE feasibility layer; Baseline 3: STGCN without synchronization constraint features
parameters.metrics: Restored load (kW), constraint satisfaction rate (%), computation time (s), synchronization safety violation count
parameters.hardware: Single workstation with GPU for STGCN inference

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Case study results demonstrate: (1) The proposed method restores 92-97% of the load restored by optimal MILP, with zero synchronization safety violations across all scenarios. (2) Computational speedup of 100-1000x over Gurobi MILP solver (0.5s vs. 50-500s per scenario), enabling real-time application. (3) The STE feasibility layer improves constraint satisfaction rate from 78% (without STE) to 99.5%. (4) Synchronization constraint features reduce MG merging failures by 85% compared to methods without explicit sync modeling. (5) The method scales well: computational time grows near-linearly with network size, while MILP time grows exponentially.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The case studies validate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed constraint-aware STGCN framework on the IEEE 123-node system. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The performance of the framework depends on several hyperparameters and design choices that need systematic tuning: the STGCN layer depth and width, the STE projection iteration count, the synchronization penalty weights (w_V, w_f, w_theta), and the warm-start curriculum schedule. The robustness of the approach under model uncertainty and communication delays also needs evaluation. [Decision] To optimize the framework and assess robustness, conducted systematic parameter tuning and sensitivity analysis. Varied the STGCN architecture parameters, STE iteration count, and penalty weights to find the optimal configuration. Also evaluated robustness under DER model uncertainty (±10% capacity variation), load forecast errors (±15%), and communication delays in the distribution management system.

parameters.architecture_search: STGCN depth: [2, 4, 6] layers; hidden dimensions: [64, 128, 256]; attention heads: [1, 4, 8]
parameters.ste_iterations: Projection iterations: [5, 10, 20, 50]; convergence criterion: constraint violation < epsilon
parameters.robustness_tests: DER capacity variation: ±10%; load forecast error: ±15%; communication delay: 0-500ms; sensor noise: 1-5% measurement error

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Optimal configuration found: STGCN with 4 layers, 128 hidden dimensions, 4 attention heads; STE with 20 projection iterations; penalty weights w_V = 1.0, w_f = 5.0, w_theta = 2.0 (frequency matching is most critical). Robustness analysis shows: (1) Performance degrades gracefully under DER uncertainty — restored load drops by <3% with ±10% capacity variation. (2) Load forecast errors have minimal impact due to the feedback-based STE layer. (3) Communication delays up to 200ms have negligible effect on solution quality. (4) The framework maintains >95% constraint satisfaction even with 5% sensor noise.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The complete framework has been designed, trained, validated on IEEE 123-node, and systematically tuned. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: To fully establish the contribution, need to isolate the impact of each component (synchronization constraints, STE feasibility layer, partial warm-start) and demonstrate the framework's ability to handle the full dynamic MG formation sequence from blackout to full restoration. Also need to verify that the system mode and system class transitions are correctly enforced throughout the restoration process. [Decision] To provide comprehensive ablation evidence, conducted detailed ablation studies and traced the complete restoration trajectory. Removed each component individually to measure its contribution. Traced the system mode evolution S^0 -> S^1 -> ... -> S^T and system class evolution C^0 -> C^1 -> ... -> C^T for the complete restoration sequence, verifying synchronization safety at each transition.

parameters.ablation_variants: Full model; w/o sync constraints; w/o STE layer; w/o warm-start; w/o temporal convolution (static GCN only)
parameters.trajectory_analysis: Trace system mode S^t and system class C^t at each step; verify all transitions satisfy synchronization safety
parameters.restoration_sequence: Full blackout -> initial MG formation -> sequential MG expansion -> MG merging -> complete restoration
parameters.visualisation: Network topology animation showing energization progress and MG boundary evolution

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Ablation results confirm each component's contribution: (1) Sync constraints: prevent 12-15 unsafe MG merging attempts per scenario that would cause voltage/frequency transients; (2) STE layer: improves feasibility from 78% to 99.5%, enabling practical deployment; (3) Warm-start: reduces training time by 60% and improves solution quality by 8%; (4) Temporal convolution: captures sequential dependencies, improving look-ahead planning by 15%. The complete restoration trajectory shows valid system mode transitions with synchronization safety verified at every step. The system class correctly contracts when approaching constraint boundaries and expands when the network stabilizes after successful MG merging.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Comprehensive validation through: (1) theoretical derivation of synchronization-safe system mode and system class framework; (2) case studies on modified IEEE 123-node feeder under multiple disaster scenarios; (3) comparison with optimal MILP (Gurobi) baseline; (4) systematic ablation studies isolating each component contribution; (5) parameter sensitivity and robustness analysis under model uncertainty and communication delays.

Restored Load Ratio.interpretation: The constraint-aware STGCN restores 92-97% of the load achieved by the optimal MILP solver, demonstrating near-optimal restoration performance while being orders of magnitude faster.

Computational Speedup.interpretation: Inference time of 0.5s compared to 50-500s for Gurobi MILP, making the approach suitable for real-time post-disaster restoration where rapid decision-making is critical.

Synchronization Safety Violation Rate.interpretation: Zero synchronization safety violations across all test scenarios, confirming that the STE-based feasibility layer and explicit synchronization constraint modeling successfully prevent unsafe MG merging operations.

Constraint Satisfaction Rate.unit: % of generated solutions are fully feasible
Constraint Satisfaction Rate.interpretation: The STE feasibility layer improves constraint satisfaction from 78% (without STE) to 99.5%, ensuring that nearly all generated restoration plans are directly deployable without manual correction.

Ablation: Sync Constraint Impact.interpretation: Explicit synchronization constraint features in the STGCN reduce MG merging failures by 85% compared to methods without sync-aware modeling, preventing 12-15 unsafe operations per scenario.

Robustness Under Uncertainty.interpretation: The framework maintains robust performance under realistic model uncertainty, with graceful degradation under DER capacity variation, load forecast errors, and communication delays.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The proposed synchronization-safe dynamic microgrid formation framework successfully addresses the critical gap in DER-led distribution system restoration by explicitly incorporating synchronization safety constraints into a learning-based optimization pipeline. The constraint-aware STGCN generates near-optimal restoration sequences (92-97% of MILP optimal) with zero synchronization safety violations, while achieving 100-1000x computational speedup over conventional MILP solvers. The STE-based differentiable feasibility layer ensures 99.5% constraint satisfaction, making the generated solutions directly deployable. Validated on the modified IEEE 123-node feeder, the framework demonstrates robustness under model uncertainty and communication delays, establishing a new paradigm for safe and efficient post-disaster distribution system restoration.
